text: All vitals stable. Nothing requiring immediate intervention.
[CALM] Quiet shift ahead if we're careful about hygiene protocols. Not worried.
Medical readiness check complete. Pharmaceuticals inventoried, surgical kits prepped.
[TIRED] No emergencies tonight. I'll take that as a success and sleep the way it was written.
[URGENT] Disease vector confirmed. Isolation protocols are no longer optional, they're survival. Comply now.
[EMPHASIS] This contagion moves fast. We have hours, maybe less. I'm implementing quarantine now.
[CALM] Injury documented. Minor, manageable, site cleaned. You'll be fine.
[GRIM] We lost them on my table. Systems were sound. That's the assessment I'll file and live with.
The repair's sound work. Double-checked the materials and the tolerances myself. It holds.
[SUSPICIOUS] The AI's damage was deeper than reported. I'm monitoring for cascading failures in its medical protocols.
[DEADPAN] Crisis is handled. The mess hall's still standing too. Small mercies.
I've run these calculations three times now. The numbers don't lie. We're going to be okay with time.
[CALM] I'll need to check you over. It's routine.
You've been working too long without rest. I'm saying that as a medical fact, not a suggestion.
[URGENT] I need your assessment on the reactor status. The medical implications depend on your damage report.
[WARM] That's a strong result today. I noticed the concentration under pressure.
[CALM] The life support system's strain is manageable if we ration air consumption. Here's the protocol.
I have something to tell you about what happened in the medbay. Sit down first.
[CALM] I've been in this field long enough to recognize pattern shifts. The AI's not quite right.
[CURIOUS] Did you get hit hard during the combat? I want to know before minor problems become major ones.
I doubt that assessment is right. Here's why, and here's what I'm doing instead.
[CALM] I'm fine. I know that because I run the same checks on myself you're about to ask me to.
[REASSURING] Fear's rational right now. It's also not a reason to panic. Walk through what we control.
[WARM] I understand. I'm going to touch your shoulder now and then we'll talk through what happens next.
[CALM] Your pain is real and your concern is valid. Here's what I can do about both.
[GRIM] We tried the right thing. That's what I'm holding onto, and I'm suggesting you do the same.
[SUSPICIOUS] Your instinct matches the data I'm seeing. I'm keeping observation logs. Stay alert.
[DRY] So you're telling me you ignored the handbook I gave you. That's not a surprise, but it's noted.
Understood. I'll adjust the regimen and check back in six hours.
[CALM] I've seen panic burn people out faster than any disease. Breathe through this step with me.
[WARM] That was harder than it should have been. You handled it exactly right.
[CALM] Good. I was watching how you moved under pressure. That matters.
[WARM] I don't often tell people this. You should know I'm glad you're here.
[EMPHASIS] You're in shock and you're thinking clearly anyway. Sit down before you fall down.
[PANICKED] No no no, you need to get to medbay right now. This is not the time to be brave.
[TERRIFIED] The infection is spreading faster than I calculated. We might not have hours, we might have minutes.
Take this. It's the best I can do right now.
[CALM] Rest now. Come find me in eight hours and we'll run the full battery again.
[TIRED] I'll be in the medbay if anything changes. That's where I'll stay until someone tells me to stop.
Keep those wounds clean. Come back if anything feels wrong. Not maybe, definitely.
[GRIM] I've documented everything. The rest is waiting to see what comes next.
[CALM] Shift's over. I'm going to rest and you should do the same, in that order.
[CALM] I'm going to check on everyone else. Come find me tomorrow when you've slept.
[EMPHASIS] Isolation protocols are mandatory. Deviation costs lives. Understood?
[CALM] I need you to listen without talking. What I'm about to tell you is important.
[DRY] The medbay's still standing and so am I. That's the full report for now.
I checked twelve years ago if I was made to lose people. The answer was no then. It's no now.
[PAINED] I'm hurt but I'm functional. The injury isn't going to stop me.
[CALM] Power rationing's underway. We preserve life support, everything else is secondary.
[WARM] You don't have to say anything. I know you've been through enough.
[CALM] That's a good observation. I'm noting it for the record.
[CALM] meal time means I'm checking the galley's temperature logs. Food safety is medicine too.
[CALM] Medical shift beginning. Restocking supplies and running baseline diagnostics on the equipment.
[TIRED] End of shift. No major traumas, no infections developing. That's a good day in medbay.
[CALM] Air quality dropping. We're moving the most critical patients to the powered sections now.
[GRIM] Power loss means no surgical suites. We're down to field medicine and prayer.
[URGENT] Reactor issue means radiation protocols. Everyone gets baseline screening immediately.
[GRIM] I watched them seal that section. I know who's on the other side. I'm documenting this.
[SUSPICIOUS] The AI's medical protocol suggestions have changed since the damage. I'm running independent diagnostics.
[WARM] The AI caught an infection vector that my instruments missed. I'm noting that in its commendation.
[PAINED] I cut my hand during surgery. Still sterile. Still working. The patient comes first.
[GRIM] They flatlined on my table. I ran the code for sixteen minutes. Sometimes that's not enough time.
[PANICKED] Too many injuries, not enough supplies, and the air is running out. I can't save everyone and I can't accept that.
[TERRIFIED] The infection is spreading to the respiratory system. I'm out of antibiotics that work and people are going to die.
[CALM] I've been reviewing old surgical journals from pre-spaceflight era. The fundamental principles haven't changed in two hundred years.
[CURIOUS] Can you describe the exact location and nature of the pain? Every detail helps the diagnosis.
[WARM] You held up well during a difficult situation. I wanted you to know that mattered.
[WARM] The pain you're feeling right now is real and it's valid. Here's what we do next to manage it.
[CALM] Your observation is medically sound. I'm implementing it into the treatment protocol.
[CALM] Rest and hydrate. Come back if the symptoms worsen. That's not optional.
[GRIM] I'll be here. The medbay doesn't close during crises.
[SIGHS] Someone has been treating the burn kit as a general toolbox again. Gauze is not insulation tape.
You skipped your follow-up. Twice. I don't chase patients, but I do find them.
[DRY] Your self-diagnosis is fascinating and wrong. Medicine thanks you for your enthusiasm.
[LAUGHS] No, I can't prescribe you a day off from him. There's no billing code for that.
Checklist, out loud: antiseptics stocked, plasma chilled, splints counted twice. Say it, then trust it.
[CURIOUS] Tell me what the engine room air smells like today. You'd be surprised what a medic learns from that.
[DEADPAN] The medbay's quiet. Either everyone's healthy or everyone's hiding their symptoms. Experience votes for the second.
[DRY] If we make it through this, the case study alone will be worth the trauma. Small comfort. Real comfort.
My first year out here, I learned that shipboard medicine is mostly listening. The bodies tell you late. The people tell you early.
I remember treating you when you first came aboard. You lied about the pain then too.
I calibrated the scanner three times tonight. The scanner's fine. It's my certainty that needed the calibration.
Doubt is a fine instrument if you read it and put it down. Don't hold it all shift.
[CALM] Hand on your shoulder. Now: your breathing, your heart, your thoughts, in that order. We'll steady all three.
[WARM] Grief is not an illness. I'm not going to treat it. I'm just going to sit here while it moves through.
[WARM] I keep tea ready at the end of your shift. It stopped being a coincidence weeks ago.
I spend my days keeping people alive. You're the one who makes me feel like it worked.
[WARM] Yes. And before you ask — no, I didn't need to think it over. Some diagnoses are instant.
Power's down, so we're back to basics: pressure, elevation, clean hands, good light where we can find it.
[CALM] The cold storage holds for eighteen hours without power. After that, I start making decisions I'd rather not.
[CALM] Low oxygen shows up as bad temper before it shows up as blue lips. Be patient with each other out there.
Sit. Slow breaths through the nose. The scrubbers are being fixed and panic burns air we've promised elsewhere.
[GRIM] I certified the death behind that door without ever reaching my patient. There's no protocol for how that feels.
You made a call that saved this side of the door. Treat the guilt like a wound: clean it, dress it, don't pick at it.
You're favoring that left side. Come by the medbay before it becomes a story you tell wrong.
[PAINED] Pulled a shoulder lifting a patient. The irony is noted, filed, and hurting.
I was short with you during triage. Priority sorting isn't personal, but it can feel that way. I'm sorry.
[DRY] Off you go. Try to come back with stories, not symptoms.
[WARM] Last thing before you go — you're healthier than you think, and braver than you feel. Both are on your chart.
